Brief.RawText: * @brief Sets the Reference Domain ID.

Lines.FullText: The Reference Domain ID.

Elements.RawText: The Reference Domain ID.

Lines.FullText: If set, gives the common identifier of one domain group.

Elements.RawText: If set, gives the common identifier of one domain group.

Lines.FullText: Signals with the same Reference Domain ID share a common synchronization source

Elements.RawText: Signals with the same Reference Domain ID share a common synchronization source

Lines.FullText: (all the signals in a group either come from the same device

Elements.RawText: (all the signals in a group either come from the same device

Lines.FullText: or are synchronized using a protocol, such as PTP, NTP, IRIG, etc.).

Elements.RawText: or are synchronized using a protocol, such as PTP, NTP, IRIG, etc.).

Lines.FullText: Those signals can always be read together, implying that a Multi Reader

Elements.RawText: Those signals can always be read together, implying that a Multi Reader

Lines.FullText: can be used to read the signals if their sampling rates are compatible.

Elements.RawText: can be used to read the signals if their sampling rates are compatible.

Description.RawText:
*
     * If set, gives the common identifier of one domain group.
     * Signals with the same Reference Domain ID share a common synchronization source
     * (all the signals in a group either come from the same device
     * or are synchronized using a protocol, such as PTP, NTP, IRIG, etc.).
     * Those signals can always be read together, implying that a Multi Reader
     * can be used to read the signals if their sampling rates are compatible.

Description.RawText:
*
     * If set, gives the common identifier of one domain group.
     * Signals with the same Reference Domain ID share a common synchronization source
     * (all the signals in a group either come from the same device
     * or are synchronized using a protocol, such as PTP, NTP, IRIG, etc.).
     * Those signals can always be read together, implying that a Multi Reader
     * can be used to read the signals if their sampling rates are compatible.

Lines.FullText: Sets the Reference Domain Offset.

Elements.RawText: Sets the Reference Domain Offset.

Brief.RawText: * @brief Sets the Reference Domain Offset.

Lines.FullText: The Reference Domain Offset.

Elements.RawText: The Reference Domain Offset.

Lines.FullText: If set, denotes the offset in ticks that must be added to the domain values of the signal

Elements.RawText: If set, denotes the offset in ticks that must be added to the domain values of the signal

Lines.FullText: for them to be equal to that of the sync source. The sync source will always have an offset of 0.

Elements.RawText: for them to be equal to that of the sync source. The sync source will always have an offset of 0.

Lines.FullText: This offset is changed only if the sync source changes and should be kept at 0 otherwise,

Elements.RawText: This offset is changed only if the sync source changes and should be kept at 0 otherwise,

Lines.FullText: allowing clients to differentiate between data loss and resync events.

Elements.RawText: allowing clients to differentiate between data loss and resync events.

Lines.FullText: Any device can choose to always keep the offset at 0, representing changes in the offset in

Elements.RawText: Any device can choose to always keep the offset at 0, representing changes in the offset in

Lines.FullText: the domain packet values instead. This implementation prevents clients from differentiating

Elements.RawText: the domain packet values instead. This implementation prevents clients from differentiating

Lines.FullText: between errors (data loss) and resync events. Additionally, if the offset is not configured,

Elements.RawText: between errors (data loss) and resync events. Additionally, if the offset is not configured,

Lines.FullText: clients have no way of detecting a resync event in the case of asynchronous signals.

Elements.RawText: clients have no way of detecting a resync event in the case of asynchronous signals.

Description.RawText:
*
     * If set, denotes the offset in ticks that must be added to the domain values of the signal
     * for them to be equal to that of the sync source. The sync source will always have an offset of 0.
     * This offset is changed only if the sync source changes and should be kept at 0 otherwise,
     * allowing clients to differentiate between data loss and resync events.
     * Any device can choose to always keep the offset at 0, representing changes in the offset in
     * the domain packet values instead. This implementation prevents clients from differentiating
     * between errors (data loss) and resync events. Additionally, if the offset is not configured,
     * clients have no way of detecting a resync event in the case of asynchronous signals.

Description.RawText:
*
     * If set, denotes the offset in ticks that must be added to the domain values of the signal
     * for them to be equal to that of the sync source. The sync source will always have an offset of 0.
     * This offset is changed only if the sync source changes and should be kept at 0 otherwise,
     * allowing clients to differentiate between data loss and resync events.
     * Any device can choose to always keep the offset at 0, representing changes in the offset in
     * the domain packet values instead. This implementation prevents clients from differentiating
     * between errors (data loss) and resync events. Additionally, if the offset is not configured,
     * clients have no way of detecting a resync event in the case of asynchronous signals.

Lines.FullText: Sets the value that indicates the Reference Time Source.

Lines.FullText: If not set to Unknown, the domain quantity is “time”, and the timestamps are absolute according

Elements.RawText: If not set to Unknown, the domain quantity is “time”, and the timestamps are absolute according

Lines.FullText: to the chosen time standard. The possible values are Gps, Tai, and Utc.

Elements.RawText: to the chosen time standard. The possible values are Gps, Tai, and Utc.

Lines.FullText: This field is used to determine if two signals with different Domain IDs can be read

Elements.RawText: This field is used to determine if two signals with different Domain IDs can be read

Lines.FullText: together. Signals that have configured a Reference Time Source are trusted to have absolute

Elements.RawText: together. Signals that have configured a Reference Time Source are trusted to have absolute

Lines.FullText: time stamps that correlate to the chosen time standard (eg. two separate PTP networks,

Elements.RawText: time stamps that correlate to the chosen time standard (eg. two separate PTP networks,

Lines.FullText: both driven through GPS can be read together, as their absolute time is the same).

Elements.RawText: both driven through GPS can be read together, as their absolute time is the same).

Description.RawText:
*
     * If not set to Unknown, the domain quantity is “time”, and the timestamps are absolute according
     * to the chosen time standard. The possible values are Gps, Tai, and Utc.
     * This field is used to determine if two signals with different Domain IDs can be read
     * together. Signals that have configured a Reference Time Source are trusted to have absolute
     * time stamps that correlate to the chosen time standard (eg. two separate PTP networks,
     * both driven through GPS can be read together, as their absolute time is the same).

Description.RawText:
*
     * If not set to Unknown, the domain quantity is “time”, and the timestamps are absolute according
     * to the chosen time standard. The possible values are Gps, Tai, and Utc.
     * This field is used to determine if two signals with different Domain IDs can be read
     * together. Signals that have configured a Reference Time Source are trusted to have absolute
     * time stamps that correlate to the chosen time standard (eg. two separate PTP networks,
     * both driven through GPS can be read together, as their absolute time is the same).
